title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: Stonehenge, the ancient stone circle in England, has (0) intrigued archaeologists and tourists for centuries. Built over several stages thousands of years ago, its true purpose remains a (1) of intense debate. Some believe it was an astronomical observatory, (2) to track the movements of the sun and moon. Others suggest it served as a sacred burial site or a place of healing. The sheer effort required to (3) these massive stones from great distances is a testament to the ingenuity of the people who built it. Without the (4) of modern machinery, they used primitive tools and sheer physical strength to arrange the monoliths in a precise circular pattern. Every year, thousands of people (5) at the site to witness the summer solstice, when the sun rises in perfect alignment with the Heel Stone. While modern technology has (6) us to learn more about the landscape surrounding Stonehenge, the deeper meaning behind the monument is still (7) in mystery. It (8) as a powerful reminder of our connection to the past and the enduring human desire to create something that outlasts time itself, standing silently on the Salisbury Plain as a global icon of ancient prehistoric engineering and spiritual mystery.

example.explanation: “Intrigued” is the most suitable verb for describing scientific or historical curiosity.

questions.explanation: “A matter of debate” is a fixed formal expression.
“Designed to [verb]” is used to describe the function of a purpose-built structure.
“Transport” is the most formal and appropriate verb for moving heavy materials over distances.
“Without the aid of” is a fixed formal phrase meaning without help from something.
“Gather at a site” is the standard way to describe a group coming together in a place.
“Enabled” is a formal way to say something made an action possible.
“Shrouded in mystery” is a fixed idiomatic collocation.
“Stands as a reminder” is a common figurative collocation for monuments.
